title: Fan Noise for Sleep — Without Running a Fan All Night
meta_title: Fan Noise for Sleep — Without Running a Fan All Night (2026)
description: Fan sound for sleep without the cold air, dust, or electricity of a real fan. Why fan hum works as a sleep cue, and how to loop gapless fan noise on your iPhone with a fade timer.
lede: If you pack a fan for vacations, this guide is for you. The part of the fan you need is the hum, and the hum fits in your pocket.
cta_tagline: Gapless fan noise, offline, with a fade timer · free download
answer_html: Short answer: the sleep benefit of a fan comes from its steady low-mid hum, which masks noise and acts as a conditioned sleep cue. A gapless fan recording reproduces both effects without cold air, dust, or the electricity draw. DRMN plays fan noise offline with a fade-out timer up to 10 hours.
body_html:
Why fan sleepers can't sleep in silence

Two things happen over years of fan sleeping. First, the hum masks household and street noise, so your sleep gets genuinely shallower without it. Second, and stronger, the sound becomes a conditioned cue: your brain has paired that exact drone with falling asleep thousands of times. Take it away in a quiet hotel room and bedtime feels wrong in a way you can't name.

Neither effect requires moving air. Recordings of a fan carry the full cue.

Real fan vs fan sound app

Real fanFan sound in DRMN
The humYesYes — gapless loop
Cold air in JanuaryYes, unfortunatelyNo
Dust and allergens circulatingYesNo
Electricity50–100 W all night (~$20–$45/year)Phone audio, screen off
Travels with youIn a suitcaseAlready in your pocket, works offline
Turns itself off gentlyNoFade-out timer up to 10 hours

How to replace your fan with DRMN

  1. Download Sound Machine Deep Sleep DRMN free from the App Store.
  2. Pick Fan from the library and download it for offline playback — hotel Wi-Fi never gets a vote.
  3. Match the volume to what your real fan sounds like from your pillow. Familiarity is the point.
  4. If the recording feels thinner than your fan, layer white noise or brown noise underneath it in the mixer.
  5. Set the timer — all night to fully replace the fan, or 60 minutes with fade-out if you only need it to fall asleep.

Transition tip: run the app and the real fan together for two or three nights, then stop the fan. The cue transfers without a fuss.
faq_html:
Why can't I sleep without a fan?

Years of pairing the hum with sleep made it a conditioned cue. The cue is the sound, so a recording reproduces it.

Is a fan sound app as good as a real fan?

For sleep, yes — masking and conditioning both come from the audio. The app skips the cold air, dust, and electricity.

Does a real fan cost much to run?

A 50–100 W box fan running nightly adds roughly $20–$45 a year at average US rates. Phone audio with the screen off is a rounding error.

faq.question: Why can't I sleep without a fan?
faq.answer: Years of falling asleep to a fan turn its hum into a conditioned sleep cue — your brain expects that exact sound at bedtime, and silence feels wrong. The cue is the sound, not the moving air, so a recorded fan hum reproduces the effect.

faq.question: Is a fan sound app as good as a real fan for sleep?
faq.answer: For the sleep effect, yes — the masking and the conditioning both come from the sound. An app adds what a fan can't: no cold air in winter, no dust circulation, no electricity draw, a fade-out timer, and the same hum in any hotel room.

faq.question: Does running a fan all night cost much electricity?
faq.answer: A typical box fan draws 50–100 watts; eight hours a night for a year adds roughly $20–$45 to an electricity bill at average US rates. A phone playing fan sound with the screen off uses a small fraction of that.
